tam_reasoning: Verified AI inference is a significant and growing market. zk-ML enables trustless model verification which is valuable for decentralized AI applications.

tam_reasoning: Digital marketing and traffic generation is a large market, but the specific niche of 'share links on social media for rewards' is a small slice of it. The AI component is minimal — it's mostly a click attribution system.
moat_reasoning: Weak moat — the link redirect and click logging service is simple to replicate. No novel consensus or verification mechanism. The 'AI marketing agency' framing doesn't translate to technical defensibility. No significant code or infrastructure.
execution_reasoning: Minimal execution: 1 Python file (127 lines), 10 TypeScript files (668 lines for ai-agent-kit), no tests. The repo is mostly empty — just a link service and some character/agent TypeScript files. The README says 'More components will be published here as the network opens up,' suggesting this is very early stage.

name: Hone
summary: ARC-AGI-2 benchmarking subnet. Miners submit git repos containing solvers, validators run them in secure GPU sandboxes. Scoring based on exact_match_rate on ARC reasoning problems. Exponential decay rewards, submission caching, daily limits. 2.7M lines of Python.
necessity_reasoning: Decentralized AGI benchmarking via ARC is a reasonable Bittensor use case — harnesses competitive pressure for a hard, well-defined problem.
tam_reasoning: ARC-AGI is a benchmark, not a product market. The TAM is indirect — value comes from advancing reasoning capabilities, not direct revenue.
moat_reasoning: Tied to the ARC-AGI benchmark specifically. Sandbox runner infrastructure and scoring system are well-built, but benchmark-specific moats are thin.
execution_reasoning: Excellent codebase. 2.7M lines Python, well-structured with miner/validator/sandbox/telemetry/tools. Docker sandboxing with GPU isolation, vLLM support. 14 GitHub stars. Professional-grade.
verdict: Solid — Well-built benchmarking subnet with strong execution, but limited direct market application.

name: Swap
summary: Incentivizes miners to provide liquidity to TAO<>USDC pool on TaoFi. Miners scored based on fees their LP positions received in past 24 hours. Simple, focused concept. 33 GitHub stars.
necessity_reasoning: Liquidity provision for TAO/USDC is a DeFi use case that doesn't clearly need Bittensor's subnet model — could be done with standard DeFi incentives.
tam_reasoning: TAO/USDC liquidity pool is limited to the Bittensor ecosystem trading volume. Moderate but not large.
moat_reasoning: No moat. Simple LP incentive mechanism. Anyone could replicate this with standard yield farming. The only differentiator is Bittensor emission rewards.
execution_reasoning: Clean, simple codebase. 78K lines Python, well-structured (neurons, swap, scripts, docs). uv-based, Docker support, 33 stars. README is minimal but the code is functional.
verdict: Avoid — Simple DeFi LP incentive that doesn't need to be a subnet, no moat.

name: Compute Horde
summary: Transforms untrusted GPUs into trusted compute for Bittensor validators. Miners spawn multiple executors (scales beyond 256-UID limit). Hardware classes (A6000, A100). Python SDK for subnet integration. Collateral-based trust for organic jobs. DDoS shield. Designed to be the compute layer for other subnets. 24 stars.
necessity_reasoning: Compute infrastructure for Bittensor is foundational — other subnets need GPU compute for validation. This is one of the most necessary subnets in the ecosystem.
tam_reasoning: Decentralized compute is a massive market. By serving other Bittensor subnets, TAM scales with the entire ecosystem.
moat_reasoning: First-mover in cross-subnet compute. SDK integration with other subnets creates network effects. Executor model scales beyond UID limits. Collateral-based trust is a strong mechanism. Well-established (24 stars, long history).
execution_reasoning: Excellent. 2.1M lines Python, very well-structured (compute_horde, compute_horde_sdk, executor, facilitator, miner, validator). SDK, docs, local_stack, tests. CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, GEMINI.md. Professional team (backend-developers-ltd).
verdict: Strong — Foundational compute infrastructure for Bittensor with network effects and excellent execution.

name: Cacheon
summary: LLM inference server optimization arena. Miners submit containerized inference servers, validators evaluate against vLLM baseline on same hardware. Fastest correct server wins 80% of emissions, runner-up 20%. V1: Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct on 8-GPU TP=8 pods. On-chain commitment with submission fees. Challenger margin system.
necessity_reasoning: Inference optimization is a strong Bittensor use case — aligns with the network's core mission of producing useful digital commodities. Inference speed directly matters for AI deployment.
tam_reasoning: LLM inference optimization is a massive market. Every AI company cares about inference speed and cost. vLLM baselines are industry standard.
moat_reasoning: The arena model (beat vLLM baseline on same hardware) is clean but replicable. The 1% challenger margin prevents churn. Moat comes from being first with this specific approach.
execution_reasoning: Good codebase. 662K lines Python, well-structured (api, cacheon_db, miner, validator, scripts, tests). Docker-based evaluation, S3 storage, on-chain commitment flow. Professional docs site (cacheon.ai/docs). 5 stars.
verdict: Strong — Clean inference optimization arena with clear value proposition and good execution.

name: Mainframe
summary: First decentralized science (DeSci) subnet on Bittensor. Simulates protein molecular dynamics via OpenMM and protein-ligand docking using DiffDock for drug discovery. 162K proteins folded since launch, 132K nsec/day simulation speed, ~17 petaflops. Partnered with Rowan Scientific for neural network potential models. Built by Macrocosmos (Steffen Cruz, former OpenTensor CTO). 30 stars.
necessity_reasoning: Decentralized scientific computing is a strong Bittensor use case — drug discovery requires massive compute that can be distributed. The subnet aligns incentives for useful scientific computation.
tam_reasoning: Drug discovery and computational chemistry is a massive market (pharma R&D spends billions). DeSci is growing. Mainframe has real-world partnerships (Rowan Scientific, Forbes coverage).
moat_reasoning: First DeSci subnet on Bittensor. Real-world partnership with Rowan Scientific. Former OpenTensor CTO as co-founder. 162K proteins folded, 17 petaflops of actual compute delivered. Strong track record and credibility.
execution_reasoning: Excellent. 435K lines Python, well-structured (folding, folding_api, neurons, tests). CircleCI, Poetry, rqlite integration. Production app (app.macrocosmos.ai). Real stats, real partnerships, Forbes coverage. 30 stars.
verdict: Strong — Leading DeSci subnet with real partnerships, proven output, and strong team.

name: Perturb
summary: Adversarial robustness testing subnet. Miners generate perturbations (adversarial examples) and defenses, validators evaluate robustness. Neural network security testing through competition. PM2 process management, GPU support. Includes task_generator and neurons structure.
necessity_reasoning: Adversarial robustness is a legitimate AI security concern. Bittensor competition could drive better defenses, but the market for this is research-oriented.
tam_reasoning: AI security/robustness testing is a growing but still niche market. Primarily academic/research value.
moat_reasoning: The adversarial competition format is somewhat novel but the concept is replicable. No proprietary data or models.
execution_reasoning: Moderate codebase. Python with neurons, perturbnet, task_generator, scripts. PM2 setup, GPU requirements. README is detailed (11K chars) but code structure is basic. Setup scripts are functional but not polished.
verdict: Weak — Interesting academic concept but niche market and moderate execution.

name: Ralph
summary: Decentralized autonomous AI research subnet. Miners propose patches to a canonical training recipe, network trains under fixed conditions, scores on a public ladder, crowns best recipe as 'king'. Produces canonical training recipe, public knowledge corpus (ralph-diffs), and demonstration model lineage. Uses TDX + nvtrust attestation. Live on mainnet.
necessity_reasoning: Decentralized AI research with verified training improvements is a strong Bittensor use case — harnesses distributed search over hyperparameters and training strategies.
tam_reasoning: AI training optimization is valuable but the market is indirect. The output (recipes, knowledge corpus) is a public good, hard to monetize directly.
moat_reasoning: The king-change protocol with hardware attestation, noise floor measurement, and public diff history creates a unique moat. Two-repo architecture (protocol + recipe) is well-designed.
execution_reasoning: Excellent. 1.6M lines Python, well-organized (eval, validator, proof, calibration, miner, chain_layer, dashboard). Live on mainnet with documented phases. WandB integration, Streamlit dashboard, TDX attestation. Whitepaper v1.3.
verdict: Strong — Well-executed autonomous AI research network with novel king-change protocol.

name: Graphite
summary: Decentralized network for solving graph optimization problems. Miners compete on NP-hard graph problems. Well-documented with philosophy sections, contribution guidelines, style guides. 14 stars. Website at graphite-ai.net.
necessity_reasoning: Graph optimization is a legitimate problem domain but doesn't clearly need a Bittensor subnet. Similar to CliqueAI — these are algorithmic competitions that could run on any platform.
tam_reasoning: Graph optimization has applications in logistics, networking, scheduling but the market for decentralized graph solving is unclear.
moat_reasoning: No clear moat. Standard optimization competition format. The documentation is thorough but the concept is replicable.
execution_reasoning: Decent codebase. 510K lines Python, well-organized (graphite, neurons, scripts, tests, verify). Style guides, contribution docs, development workflow. But the actual problem-solving approach is generic.
verdict: Avoid — Graph optimization doesn't need a subnet, generic competition format.

name: Dojo
summary: Decentralized competitive GAN (Generative Adversarial Network) on Bittensor. Miners act as both generators and discriminators in zero-sum competition. V2 challenges miners to create outputs superior to high-quality baselines, not just mimicking. Browser-based mining (Talisman wallet). Go-based codebase.
necessity_reasoning: Competitive GAN on Bittensor is creative but the use case is unclear — what specific output market does this serve? Image generation? Data augmentation?
tam_reasoning: Generative AI market is large but the GAN-competition angle is narrow and GANs are being superseded by diffusion models.
moat_reasoning: The zero-sum competitive GAN concept is novel but not clearly defensible. Browser-based mining is interesting for accessibility.
execution_reasoning: Go codebase (129K lines), well-structured with cmd, internal, docs. Lefthook git hooks, golangci-lint, Makefile. Docker support. Tensorplex Labs is a known team. But the concept is vague.
verdict: Weak — Creative but vague concept. GANs are declining vs diffusion, unclear market application.

name: engy
summary: Verified inference for frontier open models on consumer GPUs. Uses Merkle root pinning and cryptographic commitment schemes to verify model identity without TEEs. Miners commit to activations, auditors challenge samples. Light validator syncs signed epoch results from provider API.
necessity_reasoning: Verified inference without trusted hardware is genuinely needed — most inference subnets rely on trust. Merkle root + commitment proofs add real value.
tam_reasoning: Consumer GPU inference is a growing market. Serving frontier open models on cheaper hardware with verification has real demand.
moat_reasoning: The cryptographic verification scheme (Merkle roots, activation commitments, auditor challenges) is a technical moat. Hard to replicate the proof system.
execution_reasoning: Clean repo with specs, validator code, Docker setup, test suite. ~25K lines Python. Professional structure with CI/CD. Good but source still partially closed (protocol/verifier opening soon).
verdict: Interesting — Strong technical approach to verified inference, but code still partially closed.

name: greevils
summary: Participant CLI for Greevils: package & encrypt trading agents, submit to be built, deploy to confidential TEE (Google Confidential Space TDX), claim Hyperliquid account on-chain. Agents trade on Hyperliquid from within TEEs. Symmetric encryption, agent identity via SHA256, on-chain deployment.
necessity_reasoning: TEE-based trading agent deployment is a niche use case. The Bittensor subnet connection is indirect — it's really an agent deployment platform that happens to use Bittensor incentives.
tam_reasoning: On-chain trading agents on Hyperliquid is a small, speculative market. Limited to Hyperliquid ecosystem.
moat_reasoning: TEE + on-chain trading is somewhat novel but the approach is replicable. The CLI is well-built but the moat depends on the backend infrastructure (greevils-api, not public).
execution_reasoning: CLI is well-documented (9K chars README), clean Python code (50K lines). Good workflow: package -> submit -> deploy. But only the CLI repo is public — backend and validator not visible.
verdict: Weak — Niche trading agent deployment, limited TAM, only CLI repo public.

name: Ridges
summary: Open source agent competition platform on Bittensor. Miners compete and collaborate on software engineering agent tasks. Validators pull submitted code and run it on benchmark problems. Highest-scoring agent earns emissions. Supports OpenRouter, Targon, Chutes providers. Harbor sandbox execution. 85 GitHub stars.
necessity_reasoning: Software engineering agent evaluation is a reasonable Bittensor use case, but overlaps with Ditto (118) and Hone (5) in the benchmarking space.
tam_reasoning: AI coding agents are a massive market. Benchmarking and ranking them has real value for the ecosystem.
moat_reasoning: 85 stars suggests community traction, but the concept is replicable. Harbor sandbox is well-built but not unique. Platform dependency on OpenRouter for inference.
execution_reasoning: Excellent codebase. 1.15M lines Python, well-structured with API, DB, inference_gateway, miners, models, queries, validator. Alembic migrations, Docker Compose, pre-commit hooks. Professional CLI tooling.
verdict: Solid — Well-built agent competition platform with strong execution and community traction.

name: Herald
summary: Verified media placement subnet. Miners are PR operators who get news articles published in real outlets, then submit URLs. Validators run automatic public-web verification oracle (commit-reveal, outlet tier, URL live, real-news check, topic match, search index). 30-day vesting with slashing for disappeared articles.
necessity_reasoning: Verified media placement is a very niche use case that doesn't clearly benefit from being a Bittensor subnet. PR placement is a service business, not a digital commodity.
tam_reasoning: PR and media placement market is moderate but the subnet-specific angle (verified placement via on-chain incentives) is very narrow.
moat_reasoning: The verification oracle is interesting but the concept is easily replicated. The outlet registry is centrally managed, creating centralization risk.
execution_reasoning: Good code quality. 568K lines Python, well-organized (oracle, checks, attribution, commit_index, vesting, slashing, reward). Docker, deploy templates, production hardening. Commit-reveal mechanism is well-designed.
verdict: Avoid — Niche PR placement concept that doesn't clearly need to be a subnet, despite decent code.

name: Liquidity
summary: Liquidity mining system for Bittensor ecosystem. Token holders vote on liquidity pools (weighted voting with Ed25519 signatures), server calculates miner weights based on token holdings and liquidity positions. Validators fetch weights from server and set on-chain. Miners provide liquidity to pools and register EVM addresses. TypeScript-based.
necessity_reasoning: Liquidity provision for the Bittensor ecosystem is a meta-use-case — using a subnet to incentivize liquidity for other Bittensor tokens. Somewhat circular logic.
tam_reasoning: Bittensor ecosystem liquidity is moderate but growing. Limited to the Bittensor token ecosystem.
moat_reasoning: Centralized server calculates weights — validators just fetch and submit. The concept is simple and replicable. Weight verification feature adds some auditability.
execution_reasoning: Decent TypeScript codebase (111K lines). Bun runtime, Just commands, Hardhat config, Docker Compose. Weight verification script for independent auditing. But the architecture is centralized (server-dependent).
verdict: Weak — Centralized liquidity mining with circular Bittensor-specific value proposition.

name: CliqueAI
summary: AI-powered maximum clique solver network. Four-stage mechanism: problem selection (AI curates graph problems), miner selection (difficulty-adjusted sampling), scoring (optimality + diversity), weight setting (EMA). Maximum clique is an NP-hard graph optimization problem.
necessity_reasoning: Maximum clique solving is an extremely narrow academic problem. There's no clear reason this needs a Bittensor subnet — it's a well-studied problem with existing solvers.
tam_reasoning: The market for maximum clique solving is tiny. Graph optimization has applications but clique specifically is too narrow.
moat_reasoning: No moat. Standard NP-hard solver competition with EMA scoring. No proprietary technology or data.
execution_reasoning: Basic codebase. 117K lines Python, minimal structure (CliqueAI, common, docs, test_data). Shell scripts for starting miners/validators. No tests visible. Generic template-like quality.
verdict: Avoid — Too narrow academic problem, no clear need for a subnet, weak execution.

name: KubeTEE AI Factory
summary: Confidential compute AI factory on decentralized Kubernetes. Uses Kata Containers + Confidential Containers (CoCo) for TEE-based AI workload execution. Scheduled across miner clusters by Armada (CNCF multi-cluster K8s scheduler). Supports Intel TDX/SGX, NVIDIA GPU optimization. FIPS-140-3 target. NVIDIA Inception member.
necessity_reasoning: Confidential computing for AI is a genuine need — privacy-preserving inference and training. Bittensor subnet can coordinate decentralized TEE infrastructure.
tam_reasoning: Confidential computing market is growing rapidly, especially for AI workloads with sensitive data. Enterprise demand is strong.
moat_reasoning: Deep infrastructure moat: Kata Containers + CoCo + Armada + Intel TDX/SGX integration. NVIDIA Inception membership. Direct engineering collaboration with Intel and NVIDIA. Hard to replicate this stack.
execution_reasoning: Good codebase. 157K lines Python, well-documented (15K char README). Docker Compose, validator Dockerfile, .serena config, CLAUDE.md, markdownlint. Professional infrastructure approach. But solo developer (Pierre from Cyprus).
verdict: Strong — Deep infrastructure approach to confidential computing with real partnerships and technical depth.

name: cascade
summary: Building SOTA time-series foundation models (TSFM) on Bittensor. Phase 1: hold model byte-identical (Toto2-4M backbone), score data generators on synthetic data quality. Miners push data to Hippius Hub registry, commit on-chain pointers. Trainer trains king + finalist from random init. Detailed technical roadmap with citations.
necessity_reasoning: Time-series foundation models are genuinely valuable and underexplored compared to LLMs. Bittensor can coordinate the data competition efficiently.
tam_reasoning: Forecasting is a massive market (finance, supply chain, operations). TSFMs are an emerging category with broad applicability.
moat_reasoning: The fixed-model, compete-on-data approach is clever — turns synthetic data generation into the competitive surface. Deep technical roadmap with literature backing. Hard to replicate.
execution_reasoning: Excellent codebase. 1.8M lines Python, well-structured with base_generator, benchmarks, cascade core, deploy, docs, tests. CLAUDE.md, pyproject.toml, uv.lock. Thorough documentation with arXiv citations.
verdict: Strong — Novel approach to TSFM training via data competition, excellent technical depth.

name: Actual
summary: Decentralized inference layer on Bittensor. Repo is private/inaccessible (org only has a llama.cpp fork). Based on external coverage, it attempts to build decentralized inference with validator logic and miner infrastructure. Very little public information available.
necessity_reasoning: Decentralized inference is a legitimate Bittensor use case, but many subnets already compete here (Chutes, Targon, etc.).
tam_reasoning: Inference market is large but crowded. No clear differentiator visible.
moat_reasoning: No visible moat. Private repo, no public code, no clear technical differentiation.
execution_reasoning: Repo not publicly accessible. Only a llama.cpp fork under the org. Cannot assess code quality. Signals poor transparency.
verdict: Avoid — No public code, no transparency, cannot verify any claims.

name: Tag101
summary: Decentralized semantic tagging for X/Twitter posts. Miners transform posts into structured tags (entities, topics, events). Scoring: consensus (embedding clustering), validity (relevance + format), diversity (non-redundancy). Data from centralized Twitter post database with AI-account whitelist. Season-based topic expansion.
necessity_reasoning: Social media tagging is a data annotation task that doesn't clearly need a Bittensor subnet. Could be done with any crowdsourcing or ML pipeline.
tam_reasoning: Social media data annotation market is moderate but Tag101 is limited to X/Twitter posts from a curated whitelist. Very narrow.
moat_reasoning: No moat. The consensus-based scoring is standard. Centralized data source (whitelist). No proprietary data or technology.
execution_reasoning: Reasonable codebase. 205K lines Python, clean structure (chain, deploy, tasks, miner.py, validator.py, protocol.py). Docker support, pyproject.toml. Mathematical scoring formula documented. But 0 stars and centralized data dependency.
verdict: Avoid — Simple tagging task that doesn't need a subnet, narrow scope, no moat.

name: Masx.ai
summary: Bittensor ecosystem forecasting subnet. Miners run Gemini-backed forecasting agents for events (TAO price movements, subnet token prices, new registrations, governance outcomes, ecosystem metrics). Validators resolve objective ground truth, score forecasts (accuracy, confidence calibration, consistency, timeliness). Discord publishing.
necessity_reasoning: Forecasting Bittensor events is a meta-use-case. It's interesting for the ecosystem but the value is speculative — it's a prediction market for Bittensor itself.
tam_reasoning: Bittensor ecosystem prediction market is small. The forecasts are only useful to Bittensor participants, not a broader market.
moat_reasoning: No moat. Gemini-backed forecasting is just calling an API. The scoring system is standard (Brier-like). No proprietary data or models. Any prediction market could do this.
execution_reasoning: Moderate. 167K lines Python, functional structure (masxai, neurons, scripts, tests). CircleCI, .env setup. But conceptually simple — miners just call Gemini API and return structured forecasts. 0 stars.
verdict: Avoid — Speculative ecosystem forecasting with no moat, simple API-wrapper miners.

name: Claims
summary: Scientific claim-evidence extraction subnet. Miners extract claims from papers and link them to grounded evidence in source text. Validators audit for source grounding, valid claim-evidence links, and coverage. Uses ARA (Agent-Native Research Artifact) compiler skill. Agent_v1 pipeline with DSPy/LangChain/Hermes runtime options.
necessity_reasoning: Scientific claim extraction is a reasonable DeSci use case. Building a canonical claim-evidence record has research value. But could be done as a standalone service.
tam_reasoning: Scientific literature processing is a growing market (academic search, systematic reviews, evidence synthesis). Moderate TAM.
moat_reasoning: The ARA artifact model and agent pipeline provide some differentiation, but the concept is replicable. The canonical record could become a moat if widely adopted.
execution_reasoning: Good codebase. 974K lines Python, well-structured (miner/agent_v1, miner/v0, validator, neurons, schemas). Multiple agent runtime options (DSPy, LangChain, Hermes, Codex). UV-based, tests, examples. Professional.
verdict: Decent — Well-built DeSci extraction pipeline with multiple agent runtimes, moderate market.

name: Ditto
summary: Ditto is an agent benchmarking platform. The screener repo handles submission screening: downloads tarballs, builds Docker images, runs Luna source review, multi-level AI model review (Kimi K3, GPT-5.6 SOL). Validators load pre-screened images. Complex screening pipeline with quarantine mechanisms.
necessity_reasoning: Agent benchmarking on Bittensor is reasonable but overlaps with Ridges (62) and Hone (5). The screening/security focus is somewhat differentiated.
tam_reasoning: AI agent evaluation is a growing market but the Bittensor-specific angle limits TAM.
moat_reasoning: Multi-level AI screening pipeline is somewhat novel, but the approach is replicable. The screening worker is platform-operated, creating centralization risk.
execution_reasoning: Well-structured code (923K lines Python). Multi-stage screening with SOL adjudication, Docker image verification, signed verdicts. Professional setup with uv, ruff, mypy. But repo is private/screener only.
verdict: Decent — Strong screening pipeline, but concept overlaps with existing agent benchmarking subnets.

name: CookingTAO
summary: No public README or code available. The org has only two repos: 'assets' (empty) and 'Incentive-Mechanism' (contains only a PDF whitepaper draft). No miner, validator, or protocol code is public.
necessity_reasoning: Cannot assess — no public information about what this subnet does beyond the name suggesting cooking-related content.
tam_reasoning: Unknown — no public documentation of target market.
moat_reasoning: No visible moat. No code, no documentation, only a PDF whitepaper.
